title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
text:
Domain: Time Series
  Industry: Oil & Gas
  Keywords: Time Series, TimesFM, Zero-Shot Forecasting, XGBoost
  Updated: Jun 2026

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: Why gasoline is a different kind of problem
text:
Why gasoline is a different kind of problem
Energy demand has a reliable structure: daily peaks, weekly rhythms, seasonal swings. Gasoline demand doesn’t. There’s no meaningful hour-by-hour cycle — the data is weekly — and the annual seasonality, while real, is subtle enough to be obscured by week-to-week noise. On top of that, consumption is heavily influenced by factors a time series model simply cannot see: fuel prices, macroeconomic conditions, driving behavior, and — as 2020 demonstrated — global pandemics.
This makes gasoline a harder test for a zero-shot foundation model than energy demand. The question is the same: can TimesFM 2.5 outperform a naive baseline without any domain-specific training? But the bar is lower, the signal is weaker, and the failure modes are more likely to be structural.

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: The dataset
text:
The dataset
The data is U.S. Finished Motor Gasoline Product Supplied, published weekly by the EIA — 835 weeks from January 2010 through December 2025. Product supplied is a proxy for actual consumption: it measures how much gasoline leaves the distribution system each week.

The series shows a long-term plateau around 8,500–9,000 thousand barrels per day, a sharp drop during COVID-19 in 2020, and a modest recovery after. Annual seasonality exists — summer peaks are visible — but week-to-week variation is high enough to make the pattern hard to exploit without the right context window.

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: Finding the right context without a natural cycle
text:
Finding the right context without a natural cycle
In the energy project, context length was derived mathematically: align to the LCM of the patch size and the series’ natural cycles. Gasoline demand has no clean patch-aligned cycle to work from, so a different approach was needed — sweep across context lengths empirically and look for the elbow where gains stabilize.

The elbow is at 156 weeks — roughly 3 years. Scaled MAE drops sharply from 52w to 156w, then flattens. Going beyond 3 years adds memory overhead without meaningful improvement, and risks pulling in older regimes — pre-shale boom, pre-COVID — that no longer reflect current consumption patterns.

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: Does it beat the naive baseline?
text:
Does it beat the naive baseline?
The naive baseline for a weekly series is lag-52: repeat the same week from the previous year. It’s a strong baseline for seasonal data — and gasoline has enough annual seasonality to make it hard to beat consistently.
TimesFM was evaluated on three consecutive years of out-of-sample data, with no retraining between years:



Year
Model
MAPE
Scaled MAE




2023
Naive lag-52
4.06%
1.000


2023
TimesFM 156w
3.34%
0.820


2024
Naive lag-52
3.73%
1.000


2024
TimesFM 156w
2.88%
0.775


2025
Naive lag-52
3.02%
1.000


2025
TimesFM 156w
2.79%
0.920



Three consecutive years, all below 1.0. For a zero-shot model on a series dominated by external factors, that’s a stronger result than expected.

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: Can a residual model add value?
text:
Can a residual model add value?
After establishing the TimesFM baseline, the natural next question is whether a residual correction model can reduce the remaining error. Four candidates were evaluated on the 2014–2023 walk-forward residuals:



Model
R² (residual)




XGBoost
0.138


LightGBM
0.081


ElasticNet CV
−0.024


Lasso CV
−0.027



XGBoost explains only 14% of the residual variance. Linear models fail entirely — negative R² — suggesting the relationship between available features and TimesFM’s errors is non-linear and sparse. The ensemble still improved results:



Model
MAPE
Scaled MAE




Naive lag-52
3.73%
1.000


TimesFM 156w
2.88%
0.775


TFM + XGBoost residual
2.73%
0.735



The improvement is real but modest — a direct consequence of the low R² on residuals. The scatter below makes this visible: XGBoost predictions correlate weakly with actual residuals, and many weeks the correction moves in the wrong direction entirely.

Unlike the energy project, where the residual had a clear weather-driven structure that XGBoost could exploit, here the noise is more fundamental. The errors are driven by behavior and macro factors that calendar and lag features cannot capture.

title: U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
section: What this tells us
text:
What this tells us
TimesFM beat a strong naive baseline across three consecutive years on a noisy, externally-driven series — without retraining, without domain features, and without any knowledge of gasoline markets.
The harder finding is on the ensemble side. In the energy project, XGBoost corrected a systematic climatic bias and added measurable value. Here, TimesFM’s errors are largely unpredictable from available features. That’s not a failure of the model — it’s a property of the problem. Gasoline demand is harder to correct because the signal driving the errors isn’t in the data you have access to.
That distinction matters when deciding how to deploy foundation models in practice. For series with identifiable, correctable error structure — use the ensemble. For series where errors are macro-driven and opaque — the zero-shot model alone is likely your best option.

Code and full analysis on GitHub.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
text:
Domain: Combinatorial Optimization
  Industry: Logistics & Supply Chain
  Keywords: Vehicle Routing Problem, REINFORCE, Attention Model, Zero-Shot Transfer, Olist Brazil
  Updated: Jul 2026

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: Why routing gets hard fast
text:
Why routing gets hard fast
A vehicle routing problem is deceptively easy to state: given a depot and a list of customers with known demand, find efficient routes for a capacity-limited fleet that visits everyone exactly once. It’s also combinatorially brutal — a 20-customer instance already has more than 10^17 possible route orderings, far beyond what an exact solver can search directly. Classical solvers handle this by pruning that space; learned heuristics try to shortcut it entirely.
This project reproduces the attention-based policy from Nazari et al. (2018) end to end — architecture, REINFORCE training loop, and baseline variants — tests it at two problem sizes, and then takes it somewhere the original paper never did: real delivery addresses in São Paulo, via the Olist e-commerce dataset.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: The baseline choice is architectural, not a hyperparameter
text:
The baseline choice is architectural, not a hyperparameter
The model is a policy trained with REINFORCE, and REINFORCE needs a baseline — a reference value subtracted from the reward to compute how much better (or worse) a given route was than expected. Four variants were trained on VRP10 under identical conditions: no baseline at all, an exponential moving average of past rewards, a greedy baseline (the model’s own deterministic tour), and the Kool baseline (Kool et al., 2019) — a frozen copy of the model, updated only when a statistical test confirms real improvement.



W&B training curves — reward_mean over 10,000 epochs on VRP10





Baseline
Gap% vs OR-Tools




No baseline
84.7%


EMA
14.8%


Greedy
13.1%


Kool
17.1%



At VRP10, the problem is small enough that greedy and Kool land at essentially the same quality — the gap between them is within sampling noise. Only “no baseline” is a clear outlier. That result would suggest baseline choice barely matters, except it doesn’t hold at the next size up.



W&B training curves — reward_mean over 20,000 epochs on VRP20





Baseline
Gap% vs OR-Tools




Greedy
179.4%


Kool
19.5%



At VRP20, greedy collapses — to a policy worse than random routing. The reason is structural: the policy and its own baseline share the same weights, so the moment the policy improves, the baseline catches up with it, and the advantage signal that drives learning goes to zero before anything useful is learned. Kool’s frozen copy breaks that feedback loop, producing the classic phase-transition curve — a long flat stretch, then a sharp drop once the policy has pulled far enough ahead of its (stale) reference to get a real gradient. Greedy and Kool aren’t two points on the same curve; one of them simply doesn’t converge once the problem is big enough to matter.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: What a suboptimal route looks like
text:
What a suboptimal route looks like
Numbers say Kool’s VRP20 policy is 19.5% behind OR-Tools. Plotting the actual routes shows what that gap looks like on the ground.



Nazari (left) vs. OR-Tools (right) — VRP20, three representative instances


A single vehicle makes multiple trips from the depot, reloading between each; every color is one trip.
The model has no lookahead — it commits to the next customer one step at a time, with no way to see how that choice will affect the rest of the trip. The visible symptom is trip crossing: two trips of the same vehicle forming an X, a clear sign of suboptimality. OR-Tools consistently avoids this by routing along the perimeter of each cluster; Nazari has no such guarantee because it selects customers one at a time without lookahead into how future trips will interact. It’s the same trade-off in miniature that explains the whole project — a policy that decides fast and locally will occasionally produce a trip a human dispatcher would immediately flag as backwards.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: Speed vs. quality: the actual trade-off
text:
Speed vs. quality: the actual trade-off
None of this matters without weighing it against what you get in return. OR-Tools searches for up to 30 seconds per instance; Nazari runs a full batch in a single forward pass.



Problem
Nazari (greedy)
OR-Tools (GLS)
Speedup
Gap%




VRP10
~2 ms/instance
~5 s/instance
~2,500x
17.1%


VRP20
~8 ms/instance
~30 s/instance
~3,700x
19.5%



A ~20% longer tour for a ~3,700x faster solve. For dispatch systems making hundreds of routing calls in real time — or simulating thousands of scenarios where near-optimal is good enough — that trade often wins.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: Zero-shot on São Paulo
text:
Zero-shot on São Paulo
Every result above comes from customers scattered uniformly inside a unit square — a convenient synthetic distribution, and nothing like a real city. To measure that gap directly, I built VRP20 instances from the Olist Brazilian e-commerce dataset: ~15,500 São Paulo orders, geocoded via postal-code prefix, split into 5 natural delivery zones by K-means, with order weight mapped into the same demand range the model was trained on.



São Paulo customer clusters (K=5)


Running the VRP20 Kool model zero-shot — no retraining — against real customers in each zone:



Cluster
Model
OR-Tools
Gap%




C1 (Leste)
5.648
3.644
55.0%


C2 (Norte)
5.535
3.996
40.0%


C3 (Sul)
5.970
3.395
79.4%


C4 (Oeste)
5.294
3.307
61.4%


C5 (Centro-Sul)
6.043
3.572
70.7%


Overall
5.698
3.583
61.3%



The gap triples, from 19.5% on synthetic uniform instances to 61.3% on real customers. That’s the actual finding: São Paulo’s dense, unevenly clustered geography is a structurally different problem from the one the model was trained on, and the cost of that mismatch is measurable, not hand-wavy. Fittingly, the zone closest to a uniform spread (C2) has the smallest gap; the densest zone (C3) has the largest.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
section: What this shows
text:
What this shows
The interesting parts of this project are the two things a straight reimplementation wouldn’t surface on its own. First, the baseline is an architectural decision, not a tuning knob — it’s invisible at VRP10 and decisive at VRP20. Second, a 19.5% gap on the training distribution and a 61.3% gap on real customers are both true at once, and the difference between them is a precise measurement of what “trained on synthetic data” actually costs once it meets a real city.
Closing the second gap — training on São Paulo’s own spatial distribution via KDE, and scaling further to VRP50 — is the next phase of this work.

Code on GitHub.

Marcelo Arita

title: Portfolio Context — Marcelo Arita
text:
This file is for Claude Code only. It is gitignored and should never be committed. It documents design decisions, formatting rules, and conventions for this Quarto portfolio.




Quarto website, output to docs/ (GitHub Pages serves from there)
Theme: flatly + custom.scss
Language: English throughout (QMD content, navbar labels, meta fields)





marcelo-arita.github.io/
├── _quarto.yml
├── custom.scss
├── index.qmd              ← home page
├── profile.png            ← profile photo
├── assets/
│   ├── linkedin.png       ← social icon
│   └── github.png         ← social icon
├── CONTEXT.md             ← this file (gitignored)
├── _tmp/                  ← staging area for new content (gitignored ideally)
└── projects/
    ├── index.qmd          ← projects listing, organized by domain
    ├── timesfm-energy/
    │   ├── index.qmd
    │   └── images/
    │       ├── grid-overview.png
    │       ├── context-comparison.png
    │       ├── shap-features.png
    │       └── residual-temperature.png
    └── timesfm-gasoline/
        ├── index.qmd
        └── images/
            ├── series-overview.png
            ├── context-sweep.png
            ├── forecast-results.png
            └── residual-scatter.png
Project folder naming convention: <model-or-method>-<topic> (e.g. timesfm-gasoline, timesfm-energy). Image names must be descriptive — never image 1.png or generic names.




website:
  title: "Marcelo Arita"   ← brand link, clicks go to home (index.qmd)
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: projects/index.qmd
        text: Projects
No explicit “Home” item — “Marcelo Arita” brand already serves that purpose.




Pandoc/Quarto collapses nested HTML (divs inside <a>, spans inside divs) into a single line of escaped text unless wrapped in a raw block. Any HTML with internal structure must use a {=html} block:
```{=html}
<div class="...">
  <span>...</span>
</div>
```
Plain <div> in markdown body → broken output.
Links inside {=html} blocks must use directory paths (timesfm-gasoline/), not .qmd or .html — Quarto does not rewrite hrefs inside raw blocks.





Organized by domain sections (e.g. Time Series, NLP)
Domain label: .domain-label — 1.35rem, weight 600, dark color
Each project: <a class="project-row"> inside .project-list
Project row contains: title, meta (tech tags only), one-line description
No status badge on the index

```{=html}
<div class="domain-section">
  <div class="domain-label">Time Series</div>
  <div class="project-list">

    <a href="timesfm-energy/" class="project-row">
      <div class="project-row-title">Project Title</div>
      <div class="project-row-meta"><span>Python · TimesFM · SHAP</span></div>
      <div class="project-row-desc">One-paragraph description.</div>
    </a>

  </div>
</div>

---

## Project detail page (`projects/<name>/index.qmd`)

### Frontmatter

```yaml
---
title: "Full project title"
page-layout: article
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---


Three fields, each on its own line, wrapped in {=html}:
```{=html}
<div class="project-meta">
  <span><strong>Domain:</strong> Time Series</span>
  <span><strong>Industry:</strong> Oil & Gas</span>
  <span><strong>Keywords:</strong> Time Series, TimesFM, Zero-Shot Forecasting, XGBoost</span>
</div>

Fields: `Domain`, `Industry`, `Keywords`, `Updated` (format: `Mon YYYY`, e.g. `Jun 2026`). No Status, Type, Tech Stack.

### Images

- Stored in `images/` subfolder of the project
- Referenced as `![](images/filename.png)` — plain markdown, no `{=html}` needed
- Source images from Notion: copy to `images/` with a descriptive rename, then delete from `_tmp/`

---

## Home page (`index.qmd`)

Layout: photo + name side by side on top, bio paragraphs below in full width.

```html
```{=html}
<div class="profile-section">
  <div class="profile-top">
    <div class="profile-photo"><img src="profile.png" alt="Marcelo Arita"></div>
    <div class="profile-header-text">
      <p class="profile-name">Marcelo Arita</p>
      <p class="profile-location">📍 Japan</p>
      <div class="social-links">
        <a href="..."><img src="assets/linkedin.png" class="social-icon" alt="LinkedIn"> LinkedIn</a>
        <a href="..."><img src="assets/github.png" class="social-icon" alt="GitHub"> GitHub</a>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="profile-body">
    <p class="profile-bio">...</p>
  </div>
</div>
```
No CV link — the site itself is the CV.








Element
Value




Font
Inter, system-ui fallback


Body color
#37352f (Notion dark)


Link color
#2e75cc


h1
2.2rem, weight 700, line-height 1.25, letter-spacing -0.02em


h2
1.2rem, weight 600, border-bottom #ebebeb


Domain label
1.35rem, weight 600


Table header bg
#ebebeb with !important


Table header text
#37352f with !important


Table rows
odd = white, even = #f4f4f4, hover = #eaf0fb


Project meta bg
#f7f7f5, border #e8e8e6

Bootstrap (flatly theme) overrides require !important on table header background-color and color.
Tried and rejected: pastel blue (DAF0F7, D5E3F0) on cards/meta — felt out of place. Settled on warm light gray.





Headings: sentence case only — “Why this problem is harder than it looks”
Only proper nouns and acronyms stay capitalized: TimesFM, XGBoost, SHAP, PJM, EIA, etc.






Translating content to Portuguese — everything is in English
Putting images in a shared or loose folder — each project owns its images/
Using href="file.qmd" inside {=html} blocks — use directory/ instead
Bare <div> in markdown body for anything with nested elements — always use {=html}
Status/Type/Tech Stack in meta block — removed by owner preference
Adding a “Home” navbar item — brand name already links home
Committing CONTEXT.md or .quarto/ — both are gitignored

title: Projects
text:
Time Series
  

    
      Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
      
        Python  ·  TimesFM  ·  SHAP
      
      
        Zero-shot forecasting on 16 years of hourly PJM East grid data — beating a naive lag-24h baseline
        across all context configurations, with residual analysis revealing systematic weather-driven bias.
      
    

    
      U.S. Gasoline Demand Forecasting: A Harder Test for Zero-Shot
      
        Python  ·  TimesFM
      
      
        Zero-shot forecasting with TimesFM 2.5 on weekly EIA gasoline data —
        beating a naive lag-52 baseline across three consecutive out-of-sample years,
        plus a residual ensemble analysis with XGBoost.
      
    

  



  Combinatorial Optimization
  

    
      Reinforcement Learning for Vehicle Routing: From Paper to Real-World Logistics
      
        Python  ·  PyTorch  ·  OR-Tools
      
      
        Reproduces the Nazari et al. (2018) attention model for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem —
        showing the REINFORCE baseline choice is architectural, not a hyperparameter, and quantifying a
        3,700x speed-for-quality trade-off against OR-Tools that becomes a 61% gap on real São Paulo delivery data.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
text:
Domain: Time Series
  Industry: Energy & Utilities
  Keywords: Time Series, TimesFM, Zero-Shot Forecasting, XGBoost, SHAP
  Updated: Jun 2026

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: Why this problem is harder than it looks
text:
Why this problem is harder than it looks
Predicting electricity demand hour by hour sounds straightforward — the data is regular, the patterns are visible, and grids have been doing this for decades. But the difficulty is in the details. A single heat wave can push demand up by thousands of megawatts in hours. A holiday shifts the entire daily profile. An extreme weather event like a hurricane can collapse consumption overnight.
The standard solution is a dedicated model trained on historical data from the specific grid you care about. It works well — but it requires data, time, and ongoing maintenance for every new series you add.
I wanted to test a different approach: skip the training entirely, hand a pre-trained foundation model some recent history, and see if it could produce useful forecasts out of the box.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: TimesFM and the zero-shot premise
text:
TimesFM and the zero-shot premise
TimesFM 2.5 (Time Series Foundational Model) is a foundation model from Google Research, pre-trained on hundreds of billions of time series points across diverse domains — energy, retail, finance, weather. The premise is the same as modern LLMs: train on enough diverse data and the model learns patterns that generalize to new domains without retraining.
In practice, this means you give it a window of recent history as context, and it returns a forecast. No fine-tuning. No domain-specific features. No knowledge of what a power grid is.
The honest prior going in: a model with no energy-specific training should capture general temporal patterns reasonably well — daily and weekly cycles are not unique to power grids. Whether it could beat even a naive baseline was less obvious.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: 16 years of a power grid
text:
16 years of a power grid
The dataset is hourly load data from the PJM East grid — the electrical network serving the northeastern United States, from 2002 to 2018. Strong daily cycles, clear weekly rhythms, pronounced annual seasonality driven by heating and cooling demand.

One data quality issue worth addressing before modeling: Hurricane Sandy in October 2012 caused a sharp sustained drop in consumption across the region. Rather than letting that anomaly distort the model’s context window, those hours were replaced with historical averages for the same hour and day of week from the three prior years — preserving continuity without ignoring the event.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: How much context does the model need?
text:
How much context does the model need?
TimesFM processes input as fixed-size patches of 32 points. This has a direct implication for input design: context length should align with the series’ natural cycles to avoid cutting them mid-patch. For hourly energy data with daily (24h) and weekly (168h) cycles, the mathematically clean choice is LCM(32, 24, 168) = 672 hours — four weeks of fully aligned history.
Three context lengths were evaluated against a naive baseline — repeat the same hour from the previous day — using walk-forward validation over the first 7 months of 2018:



Context
MAPE
Scaled MAE




Naive lag-24h
7.91%
1.000


512h (~3 weeks)
4.74%
0.630


672h (~4 weeks, cycle-aligned)
4.63%
0.616


8,736h (1 year)
4.44%
0.580



Scaled MAE below 1.0 means the model beats the naive baseline. All three configurations did.
The bigger insight is in the pattern across context lengths. Cycle alignment helps modestly. The real gain comes from a full year of history — with 8,736 hours of context, the model can see that January 2018 should look like January 2017. That’s a pattern no three-week window can capture.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: Where the model fails: weather
text:
Where the model fails: weather
A 4.44% MAPE is a solid result for a zero-shot model. The more interesting question is where the remaining error comes from. Training an XGBoost model on TimesFM’s residuals — using weather and calendar features — and analyzing it with SHAP gives a clear answer.

The three most important features are all weather-derived: humidity, apparent temperature, and temperature. Calendar features barely register. TimesFM already handles temporal structure well — it has internalized weekdays, weekends, and seasonal patterns from pre-training. What it cannot account for is current weather conditions.
On days above 25°C, the mean residual is +1,331 MW — the model consistently underestimates actual consumption. Air conditioning on hot days pushes demand beyond what historical temporal patterns suggest, and there’s no mechanism in a pure time series model to capture that.

The residual vs temperature scatter makes this visible: as temperatures rise above ~22°C, errors shift systematically upward. XGBoost learned to correct for this — but the correction was modest. Adding it reduced the hot-day residual from +1,331 MW to +1,235 MW and improved Scaled MAE from 0.580 to 0.562. The bottleneck was data volume: roughly 520 hours above 25°C in the training window, too few to learn the correction reliably.

title: Energy Demand Forecasting: Testing a Zero-Shot Foundation Model on Real Grid Data
section: What this tells us about foundation models
text:
What this tells us about foundation models
TimesFM outperformed the naive baseline across all configurations — on a domain it was never explicitly trained for, with no fine-tuning and no energy-specific features. That result holds up.
The more useful finding is about failure modes. The model doesn’t fail randomly — it fails systematically, in predictable regimes. That predictability matters more than it might seem: it means errors can be diagnosed and corrected with targeted interventions rather than retraining the entire model. The ensemble added value not because XGBoost predicted residuals well (R² = 0.034) but because it corrected a consistent directional bias in a specific climate regime.
Zero-shot time series models are not a replacement for domain expertise. But as a starting point — especially when historical data is limited or you’re forecasting across many series — they’re further along than expected.
One caveat worth noting: the PJM East dataset is publicly available and widely used in the time series literature, so there is a possibility that part of this data was included in TimesFM 2.5’s pre-training corpus. For a rigorous scientific benchmark, that would be a relevant limitation. The goal here is educational — to explore model behavior, validate intuitions about input engineering, and understand where the ensemble approach adds value and where it doesn’t. Results should be interpreted in that context.

Code and full analysis on GitHub.
